coredata.dc:title: Three-dimensional carbon dots/Prussian blue analogues nanocubes /nickel foams as self-standing electrodes for high-performance hybrid electrochemical capacitors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhirong
Song, Rongbin
Zhang, Lin
Li, Zhaohui
Yao, Hongchang
Liu, Qingchao
Wang, Jianshe
Li, Zhongjun
coredata.dc:description: Developing the high-performance supercapacitors is overwhelmingly dependent on the composition design and structure tailoring of electrode materials. By a one-step solution method, the composite of carbon dots/Prussian blue analogues nanocubes-incorporated three-dimensional Ni foams was prepared and used as a self-standing positive electrode for hybrid electrochemical capacitors (HEC). Aside from the role of Ni source for Prussian blue analogues (PBA), Ni foams acts as 3D conductive supports, making electrolytes more accessible to the internal surface of electrode. Meanwhile, carbon dots can be absorbed for the formation of carbon dots/PBA nanocubes on Ni foams surfaces, offering optimized interfaces for the interactions between electrodes and electrolytes and relieving the decomposition of PBA in alkaline electrolyte. With these merits, the carbon dots/Prussian blue analogues nanocubes-Ni foams electrode in the hybrid electrolyte of 0.5 M KOH and 1.3 M Na2SO4 exhibits a maximum specific capacity of 659 C g−1 at current density of 0.5 A g−1 and 344 C g−1 even under large current density of 5 A g−1. An extended working potential window of 1.8 V, high energy density of 65 Wh kg−1 and high power density of 4.052 kW kg−1 as well as improved cyclic stability are also achieved in the assembled HEC. This study builds a boulevard to improve the application potential of PBA in HEC, which will be beneficial for the development of supercapacitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prussian blue analogues
Carbon dots
Nanocomposites
Hybrid electrochemical capacitors
Nickel foam

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assembly of the hybrid electrochemical capacitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD and (b) FTIR patterns of NiFe PBA-NF and CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF.
SEM images of (a-c) NiFe PBA-NF and (d-f) CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF at different magnifications.

Evolution of the UV–vis absorption spectra of the PBA precursor solution in time with CDs (CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF) and without CDs (NiFe PBA-NF) (the spectra were recorded 30 min, 100 min, 300 min, 480 min and 720 min after the beginning of the experiment).
(a) EDS and (b-e) TEM images at different magnifications of the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF.

The comparison of electrochemical properties of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode in three kinds of electrolytes (All the test were repeated for three times): (a) the discharge curves at 0.5 A g−1, (b) the specific capacity at different densities, (c) CV curve at 5 mV s−1, (d) EIS curves (the insets are enlarged area at high frequency and the equivalent series resistance).

Electrochemical performance of samples with different amount of CDs in 1.3 M KOH + 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte (All the test were repeated for three times): (a) CV curves at scan rate of 5 mV s−1, (b) CV curves of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF at various scan rates, (c) GCD discharge curves at current density of 0.5 A g−1, (d) the specific capacity at different current densities, (e) Nyquist curves and their enlarged views at high-frequency region, (f) the plots of log(i) versus log(v), (g) capacitive contribution at different scan rates and (h) cycle life curves of the NiFe PBA-NF and CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF at 10 A g−1 for 5000 cycles.

Electrochemical performances of the obtained HEC (All the test were repeated for three times): (a) CV curves in different potential windows at 5 mV s−1, (b) the charge and discharge curves, (c) Ragone plots and another PBA based supercapacitors reported previously, (d) cyclic performance (Inset: The fully charged HEC operates LEDs and an electric motor fan).
Schematic illustration for synthesis of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composites electrodes.
The EIS equivalent circuit fitting parameters for different electrolytes based on CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode and for different electrodes in the electrolyte of 1.3 M Na2SO4 + 0.5 M KOH.

head.ce:title: Three-dimensional carbon dots/Prussian blue analogues nanocubes /nickel foams as self-standing electrodes for high-performance hybrid electrochemical capacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Ecology and Environment, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Ecology and Environment, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhirong Guo and Rongbin Song contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing the high-performance supercapacitors is overwhelmingly dependent on the composition design and structure tailoring of electrode materials. By a one-step solution method, the composite of carbon dots/Prussian blue analogues nanocubes-incorporated three-dimensional Ni foams was prepared and used as a self-standing positive electrode for hybrid electrochemical capacitors (HEC). Aside from the role of Ni source for Prussian blue analogues (PBA), Ni foams acts as 3D conductive supports, making electrolytes more accessible to the internal surface of electrode. Meanwhile, carbon dots can be absorbed for the formation of carbon dots/PBA nanocubes on Ni foams surfaces, offering optimized interfaces for the interactions between electrodes and electrolytes and relieving the decomposition of PBA in alkaline electrolyte. With these merits, the carbon dots/Prussian blue analogues nanocubes-Ni foams electrode in the hybrid electrolyte of 0.5 M KOH and 1.3 M Na2SO4 exhibits a maximum specific capacity of 659 C g−1 at current density of 0.5 A g−1 and 344 C g−1 even under large current density of 5 A g−1. An extended working potential window of 1.8 V, high energy density of 65 Wh kg−1 and high power density of 4.052 kW kg−1 as well as improved cyclic stability are also achieved in the assembled HEC. This study builds a boulevard to improve the application potential of PBA in HEC, which will be beneficial for the development of supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: The looming energy crisis and growing environmental concerns are stimulating the rapid development of renewable energy and energy storage technologies [1,2]. In the field of energy storage devices, hybrid electrochemical capacitors (HEC) have in recent times gained significant interest owing to their outstanding potentials on electrochemical performances such as high energy density comparable to lithium ion batteries and high power density of electric double layer capacitors (EDLCs) [3,4]. HEC are generally comprised of an electric double layer or pseudo-capacitive electrode and a battery-like electrode with an electrolyte [5,6]. For this emerging energy storage device, there are a lot of works to be carried out in order to establish its prospects for practical applications that involves the development of advanced electrode materials [7–9].

Prussian blue analogues (PBA) are known as one of the classical metal organic frameworks. Their molecular formula is expressed as [A x M′ a M″ b (CN) 6 ], where A is a mobile cation such as K+ and Na+ ions; M′/M″ represent transition metal cations located at the octahedral sites which form the coordination bonds with nitrogen and carbon, respectively. The unique features of PBA such as open framework structures and abundant active sites endow them with fast ion intercalation kinetics and large theoretical specific capacity, offering a great possibility in the electrode of HEC application nowadays [10,11]. However, PBA suffer from poor conductivity and chemical stability, which severelyrestrict their practicalapplications [12]. To overcome these issues, PBA have been integrated with conductive materials, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNT) and Ni foams (NF) [13–17]. Compared to the carbon materials, the NF can release the Ni ion for the in situ growth of PBA on NF surface, which forms a more intact contact between PBA and conducting materials and acts as a self-standing electrode [18]. More importantly, the 3D macroporous structure of NF guarantees that internal surfaces of electrode are accessible by electrolyte, enlarging the electrode/electrolyte interaction interfaces. In this case, the NF emerges as a more attractive candidate to improve the performance of PBA in HEC. For example, the Ni2Co-PBA grown on NF has been developed, which have manifested enhanced electrochemical properties with a ultrahigh specific capacity of 585 C g−1 at the current density of 0.5 A g−1 [19]. However, the poor chemical stability of PBA is still existed in the PBA/NF system. Under the premise of retaining the structural advantage of PBA/NF system, adopting suitable composition modulation has become the preferred way to solve the aforementioned issue.

As a kind of typical carbon materials, carbon dots (CDs) possess the features of unique physicochemical properties, ease of synthesis and low cost [20]. Moreover, compared with multidimensional carbon materials, the zero-dimensional structure endows CDs with higher flexibility for constructing nanocomposites with solid cross-linking [21–23]. On these contexts, researches on the electrode materials of supercapacitor have begun to flourish in fabricating various CDs-based binary composites, such as CDs/NiCo2O4, CDs/polyaniline (PANI) and CDs/graphene composites [24–26]. Benefiting from their abundant surface functional groups, CDs in these researches have been found to offer excellent interfaces for the interactions between electrodes and electrolytes, eventually facilitating the improvement of capacitive properties [27]. On other level, the occupation of some electrode/electrolyte interfaces by CDs will enhance the chemical stability of electrode materials and the cycling stability of HEC due to their good chemical stability, especially when the other component in CDs-based binary complex is easily decomposed in electrolyte. Therefore, we infer that CDs have the potential to favor the improvement of chemical stability and capacitive performance of PBA/NF system from the level of composition design.

Herein, utilizing NF as both nickel source and conductive scaffold, we developed a novel three-dimensional (3D) macroporous framework of NF decorated with CDs/NiFe PBA nanocubes (NCs) via a one-step in situ solution precipitation method. The obtained CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF was used as the self-standing positive electrode for the HEC with a hybrid electrolyte of KOH and Na2SO4, manifesting higher specific capacity and enhanced cyclic stability compared to NiFe PBA-modified planar electrode. Such impressive performance can be attributed to the 3D macroporous structure provided by NF, the fine capacitive nature of PBA as well as the enhanced chemical stability and the optimized electrode/electrolyte interface from the introduction of CDs.

Potassium hexacyanoferrate II (K4[Fe(CN)6]), trisodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O), citric acid, urea and other chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Nickel foam (NF, purity of 99.8%) and activated carbon (AC) were purchased from Xinxiang Heluelida Power Materials Co., Ltd.

CDs was firstly prepared through the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid and urea according to the procedure reported in previous literature [28]. The CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite was then synthesized by a one-step solution precipitation method. Typically, 0.8 mmol of K4[Fe(CN)6], 0.5 mmol of C6H5Na3O7·2H2O and 10 mg of CDs powders were dissolved in 20 mL of 0.2 M HCl to form a green transparent homogeneous solution. Then, a piece of NF (1 cm × 1 cm) treated as previously reported [29] was immersed into the above solution for 24 h without interruption. Finally, the obtained CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite was washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times and dried at 60 °C overnight. To investigate the influence of CDs content on the morphologies of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF, CDs dose was changed to 6 and 20 mg, respectively. The as-synthesized samples are designated as CDs1/NiFe PBA-NF and CDs2/NiFe PBA-NF. For comparison, NiFe PBA-NF was synthesized with the same procedures but in the absence of CDs, while the CDs/NiFe PBA-T was prepared by the replacement of NF by the Ni salt in the similar procedure. Moreover, the NiFe PBA-T fabricated by the use of Ni salt in the absence of CDs was also used as the most basic control group.

The product powders were scraped off from the NF and examined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, X'PertPRO diffractometer) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å). FTIR spectra were measured by using KBr pellets on a Nicolet-Nexus 470 FTIR spectrophotometer. The morphologies and compositions of the samples were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TM-1000) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM2100F). UV–vis absorption spectra of the precursor solution of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF and NiFe PBA-NF were recorded at reaction times of 10 min, 30 min, 60 min, 150 min and 240 min on the Yu-1880 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The Zeta potentials were measured by Zeta potentiometer (Zen3700). TGA curves were obtained by thermogravimetric analyzer (NET-ZSCH STA 409PC/PG) with temperature in range of 30–1000 ℃ and a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1 under air atmosphere. The content of water molecules and CDs were calculated from the weight loss rate at 200 ℃ and 1000 ℃, respectively. The Ni, Fe, K, Na contents were tested by inductively coupled plasma spectrometer (ICP, Thermo Fisher 6300).

The electrochemical properties were conducted in a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China) and Land test system (LANHE, Wuhan, China) in a typical three-electrode system. To ensure the accuracy and reproducibility, electrochemical tests were carried out for three times for each sample. The as-synthesized samples with NF were directly used as the working electrodes, while the saturated calomel electrode and platinum sheet (1 cm × 1 cm) were adopted as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. For CDs/NiFe PBA-T and NiFe PBA-T, same amount of them were mixed with acetylene black and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) in a mass ratio of 8:1:1 and dropped onto NF (1 cm × 1 cm), then dried and pressed at 10 MP and finally used as working electrodes. Their contents are equal to that of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs on the NF (2 mg). To optimize the electrolyte, KOH, Na2SO4 aqueous solution and their mixture solutions with different concentrations were employed as electrolytes at an ambient temperature, respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were tested. The specific capacity of electrode material, Cs (C g−1), was calculated from the galvanostatic discharge curves according to the Eq. (1) [30,31]:
(1)
C s
=
I × t
/ m

where I, t and m represents the constant current (A), the discharge time (s) and the mass of active material of single electrode (g), respectively. For the electrode prepared by coating method (i.e. CDs/NiFe PBA-T and NiFe PBA-T), the value of m equals the weight increment (△m) on NF substrate. While for the electrode synthesized by in-situ growth on NF (i.e. NiFe PBA-NF and CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF), their masses of active material were calculated by the following Eqs, respectively.
(2)
m
PBA
= Δ m ×
M

PBA

= Δ m ×
442 383
= 1.15 × Δ m
(3)
m
CDs - P B A
= Δ m ×
M
'
PBA
( 1 + x )
M

M

= Δ m ×
445.2
386.2
= 1.15 × Δ m

Wherein, △m can be obtained by directly weighing the bare substrate and active material covered Ni foams after solution precipitation reaction. MPBA and M’PBA are relative molecular mass of PBA in samples of NiFe PBA-NF and CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF.
x =
m
CDs
m
PBA
= 5.0%, which was calculated by the weight loss rate of NiFe PBA-NF and CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF at 1000 ℃ from TGA results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of the hybrid electrochemical capacitors
ce:section.ce:para:
CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF was directly applied as the positive electrode, and AC electrode prepared according to the method described in literature [19] was used as the negative electrode. The mass ratio of positive and negative electrode was 0.51 in order to balance the charge of positive and negative electrodes. They were placed face to face and separated by a piece of polypropylene membrane for assembling the HEC [32]. The working potential window was measured by CV technique. The specific capacity (C, C g−1) of HEC was detected by GCD and calculated by the Eq. (1), but m was the sum mass of active materials on both electrodes. The energy density (E, Wh kg-1) and power densities (P, kW kg-1) were calculated by the Eq. (4–5) [33,34].
(4)
E = 0.5 × C × Δ V / 3.6
(5)
P = E × 3 . 6 / Δ t

The fabrication process of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite was depicted in Scheme 1 . In acid media, NF slowly releases Ni2+ ions, which serves as active sites for PBA nanoparticle nucleation through the coordination reaction with [Fe(CN)6]4- [35]. As soon as NiFe PBA crystal nucleuses are formed, they are capped by the citric acid anion, which endows the crystal nucleuses with a highly negative Z-potential of −26.2 mV. Meanwhile, the protonated CDs with a Z-potential of 2.03 mV tightly adhere to the surface of PBA crystal nucleuses through electrostatic adsorption. With the further growth of PBA crystal nucleuses, the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite is obtained. To characterize the compositions and morphologies, the as-obtained products were performed by XRD, FTIR, SEM and TEM measurements. In the XRD pattern of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite (Fig. 1 a), we observe five peaks located at 17.3°, 24.6°, 35.1°, 39.4° and 43.3°, which can be indexed to (200), (220), (400), (420) and (422) reflections of NiFe-PBA with face-centered cubic phase (JCPDS card No. 014–0291) [36]. In comparison to NiFe PBA-NF, CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF displays sharper diffraction peaks, revealing a better crystallinity [37]. The crystal growth is promoted due to a slowdown in the precipitation rate of PBA particles by the presence of CDs. From FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 1 b, typical features of hexacyanoferrate compounds are observed in the curves of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF and NiFe PBA-NF composites. The band centered at 2087 cm−1 is assigned to the CN group coordinated by Ni2+ and Fe2+ stretch [38]. The peak at 597 cm−1 relates to the in-plane deformation of Fe-C. The absorption bands at 3405 and 1614 cm−1 correspond to the stretching and distortion vibration mode of OH in water molecules, respectively [39]. These peaks effectively demonstrate the formation of NiⅡFeⅡ(CN)6 in the composites. Moreover, the intensities of peak at 1413 cm−1 assigned to OH bending mode of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF are stronger than that of NiFe PBA-NF [40]. As the CDs possess a lot of oxygen-contained groups on surfaces, this intensified peak reflects the introduction of CDs into the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite.

As seen from SEM images (Fig. 2 a and d), both the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF and NiFe PBA-NF display a 3D macroporous structure, indicating the inherent structure of NF is still preserved in the acid environment. In the high-magnification SEM images, unlike the presence of spherical nanoparticles clusters with irregular sizes ranging from 20 to 100 nm on the NF of NiFe PBA-NF sample (Fig. 2 b and 2 c), the CDs-decorated nanocubes with an average side length of about 100 nm are observed on NF surfaces for CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite (Fig. 2 e and 2 f). To make clear the reason for such morphology change, we varied the additive amounts of CDs for the preparation of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite. For CDs1/NiFe PBA-NF composite with a decreased amount of CDs, the morphology is similar to that of NiFe PBA-NF sample. In contrast, a cauliflower-like shape with a size range of 300–500 nm is exhibited for the CDs2/NiFe PBA-NF composite with an increased CDs content (Fig. S1). These results clearly indicate that the change on the morphology of NiFe PBA is indeed from the introduction of CDs into the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite. As previously mentioned, the CDs can be absorbed on to the surface of NiFe PBA crystal nucleuses. When the CDs content reached a certain level, the precipitation reaction would be regulated to favor a grain orientation growth, eventually leading to the formation of NiFe PBA nanocubes. However, if the overhigh CDs content was used, the crystal nucleuses should be completely wrapped by CDs. In this case, the crystals prefer an isotropically growth, and thus the formation of spherical nanoparticles. To demonstrate the influence of CDs on the precipitation reaction rate, we monitored the reaction solutions by UV–vis spectrum. As shown in Fig. 3 , both the reaction solutions for the preparation of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF and NiFe PBA-NF composites show a broad band centered at 850 nm, which is related to the PBA particles [41]. However, at any reaction time, the peak intensities of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite are always lower than these of NiFe PBA-NF composite, indicating that the presence of CDs can slow down the formation rate of NiFe PBA.

The EDS mapping images of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite reveals an even distribution of C, N, O, Ni and Fe elements with atomic percentages of 37.5, 16.6, 2.4, 2.1 and 3.1%, respectively (Fig. 4 a). Accompanied by TGA and ICP analysis (Fig. S2, Table S1), the chemical formulas of NiFe PBA-NF and CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composites are determined to be
N
a
0.6
K
6.0
N i
( II )
[ F e
( II )
( C N )
6
]
0 . 34
□
0 . 66
· 3.5
H
2
O
and
1.8 C D s · N
a
0.25
K
5.8
N i
( II )
[ F e
( II )
( C N )
6
]
0 . 40
□
0 . 60
· 2.7
H
2
O
respectively, in which □ stands for [Fe(CN)6]4− vacancies. Clearly, the amount of vacancies is decreased after the addition of hydrophilic CDs, which means fewer water molecules in the interstitial channels or the vacancies, and thus a higher stability of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite. From the obtained TEM image of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite in Fig. 4 b-c, we can clearly observe that the monodispersed CDs with diameters of 5 nm are evenly combined with NiFe-PBA nanocubes. Their lattice fringes can be observed from the HRTEM images (Fig. 4 d-e). The (200) plane with d interval of 0.59 nm, (220) plane with d interval of 0.37 nm and (222) plane with d interval of 0.29 nm belong to PBA, while the lattice fringe with spacing of 0.27 nm corresponds to CDs [42,43]. All of these characterizations demonstrate the successful fabrication of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite.

We investigated the capacitive properties of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrodes with different CDs contents (Fig. S3). Obviously, both the discharge times and CV integral areas are initially increased and then decreased when the CDs contents are increased. As the CDs can provide excellent interfaces for the electrolytes/electrodes interaction, the increase in the CDs amount can firstly improve the specific capacity. However, when the CDs amount is over high, the electrolytes/electrodes interfaces would be completely occupied by the CDs. Since the CDs have very small capacities themselves, the specific capacity decreases with the further increase of CDs amounts. Thus, the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode with a suitable CDs amount displays the best specific capacity.

To manifest the superiority of the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode, the capacitive properties of NiFe PBA-NF electrode were also evaluated for comparison. Moreover, the CDs/NiFe PBA-T and NiFe PBA-T prepared by directly using Ni salt as Ni source were also used as control groups. The SEM images reveals the structures of CDs/NiFe PBA-T and NiFe PBA-T are similar to those of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF and NiFe PBA NCs-NF, but without the 3D macroporous structure, respectively (Fig. S4). Fig. 6 a shows the CV curves of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF, NiFe PBA-NF, CDs/NiFe PBA-T, NiFe PBA-T and NF electrodes at a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1 in a potential window of 0–0.55 V. NF shows a negligible contribution to the total capacity from its flat CV curve. The calculated specific capacity of NF is only 0.02C g−1 at 5 mV s−1. All the other CV curves have pronounced redox peaks, corresponding to the Faradaic reactions of FeII/FeIII [46]. Moreover, the shape of CV curves in the voltage range of 0–0.55 V for CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode from scan rate of 5 mV s−1 to 50 mV s−1 retains very well, suggesting fast charging/discharging kinetics (Fig. 6 b). The voltage platform appeared in each galvanostatic discharge curve is the feature of battery‐type electrode materials (Fig. 6 c). Among the four samples, CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode has the longest discharge time and largest CV integral area. The calculated specific capacity values from GCD curves at different current densities are shown in Fig. 6 d. At any current density, the specific capacities of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode (e. g. 659 and 344 C g−1 at 0.5 and 5 A g−1, respectively) are superior to these of NiFe PBA-NF electrode (e. g. 318 and 137 C g−1 at 0.5 and 5 A g−1, respectively), which can be attributed to the enhanced conductivity and electrolytes/electrodes interaction provided by the introduction of CDs. Similarly, the specific capacities of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode are also higher than those of CDs/NiFe PBA-T electrode (e. g. 329 and 143 C g−1 at 0.5 and 5 A g−1, respectively), indicating that the extend electrochemical active area and the accelerated electrolyte transport originated from the 3D macroporous structure of NF have indeed improve the capacitance properties. The specific capacities of NiFe PBA-T electrode (e. g. 221 and 101 C g−1 at 0.5 and 5 A g−1, respectively) are inferior to those of CDs/NiFe PBA-T electrode, further confirming the positive effect of CDs on the capacitance property. Moreover, the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode material was also compared with other reported PBA materials. As listed in Table S3, the specific capacity of the sample in our work is higher than that of many reported PBA electrode materials.

The impedance data in Fig. 6 e were fitted based on the same equivalent circuit, and the corresponding parameters are listed in Table 1. As shown in the enlarged view at high frequency region and Table 1, the Rs of the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode is significantly lower than those of NiFe PBA-NF and CDs/NiFe PBA-T electrodes, further highlighting the indispensability of both CDs and NF in the acceleration of carriers migration during the charging and discharging process [47,48]. To investigate the charge storage mechanisms, a semi-quantitative analysis was performed from the CV curves. Fig. 6 f shows the relationship between the anodic peak current (i) and scan rate (v), which is investigated by the following Eq. (6) [49]:
(6)
l o g i = b × l o g v + l o g a

When the value of b is equal to 1, it indicates surface-limited kinetic reaction; while when the b value is 0.5, it is diffusion-controlled process [50]. The b values of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF, NiFe PBA-NF, CDs/NiFe PBA-T and NiFe PBA-T electrodes are 0.57, 0.52, 0.45 and 0.39, respectively. It can be concluded that these composites are a battery-like electrode material with diffusion-controlled electrode reaction. However, the co-existence of CDs and NF leads to the formation of surface-limited reactions at the interfaces between the electrolyte and these electrodes. To further explore the contribution of diffusion and capacitive process, the current (i) could be expressed by the following Eq. (7) [51,52]:
(7)
i
V
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2

where i (V) is the current at a fixed voltage, v is the scan rate, k1 and k2 are the constants corresponding to the capacitive and diffusion-limited processes, respectively. Fig. 6 g displays the percentage of surface capacitive contribution of the sets of electrodes at different scan rates. With increase of the scan rate to 50 mV s−1, the capacitive contribution rate of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF increases to more than 50%, which is the highest value among all tested electrodes. Such enhancement should be a result of the synergistic effect between the surface properties of CDs and the space structure of NF, powerfully demonstrating the effectivity of our design.

The cycling stability of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF and NiFe PBA-NF electrodes were compared in Fig. 6 h. Under the charge/discharge current density of 10 A g−1, the capacity of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode keeps stable at first 500 cycles and then slowly decreases. After 3000 cycles, it retains 60% of its maximum capacity, higher than that of the NiFe PBA-NF electrode (49%). The XRD tesets of these two electrodes were also performed after 5000 cycles to probe the enahced stablility of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode (Fig. S5). Undoubtedly, the formation of hydroxides occurs on the surfaces of both CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF and NiFe PBA-NF electrode due to the decomposition of NiFe-PBA in hybird electrolyte. However, we can observe that the characteristic peaks of hydroxides on the XRD pattern of NiFe PBA-NF are more pronounced and sharper compared to those of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF, indicating a heavier decomposition during the charge/discharge test. Thus, the improved cyclic performance of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode is due to the presence of CDs on electrolyte/electrode interface, which can reduce the exposure of NiFe PBA towards KOH electrolyte and thus retard the etching of electrode material. Taken together, the superior performance of CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF can be ascribed to the following aspects. Firstly, the conductive NF as skeleton ensures the good electrical contact of NiFe PBA NCs and enlarges the electrochemical active areas. Secondly, the coverage of CDs can not only maximize the conductivity of NiFe PBA NCs but also protect the NiFe PBA NCs from etching of electrolyte. Thirdly, cubic morphology and better crystallinity contribute to open framework structures and abundant active sites, which facilitate ion transport intercalation kinetics [10].

The CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF//AC HEC were assembled by using CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF as positive electrode and AC as negative electrode, which were tested in the hybrid electrolyte for practical utilization. The operating voltage of HEC is set at 1.8 V since the obvious polarization current peak appears until the potential exceeds 1.8 V (Fig. 7 a). From the GCD curves in Fig. 7 b,the specific capacity is 225 C g−1 at 1 A g−1. The ultrahigh energy density of 65 Wh kg−1 can be obtained at power density of 405 W kg−1. When the power density reaches to 4052 W kg−1, the energy density is still as high as 30 Wh kg−1 (Fig. 7 c). The E-P performances are outstanding compared with most of the reported PBA based HEC, such as NiCo-PBA//AC in 3 M KOH (39.4 Wh kg−1 at 400 W kg−1), Graphene oxide (GO)@PB//AC in 0.5 M Na2SO4 (20.1 Wh kg−1 at 450 W kg−1), CoFe-PBA//AC in 1 M Na2SO4 (17.4 Wh kg−1 at 136 W kg−1) and NiCo-PBA@polypyrrole (PPy)//AC in 2 M KOH (21.7 Wh kg−1 at 888 W kg−1) [19,53–55]. In addition, the HEC exhibits long-term lifespan with a capacitance retention of approximately 60% after 2000 cycles (Fig. 7 d). These outstanding performances greatly support the upgradation of PBA from both composition modulation and structure tailoring. The HEC was also tested to light up the light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and drive an electrical motor fan (in the inset of Fig. 7 d).

In summary, the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF composite was successfully developed by a one-step in situ solution precipitation method and applied as the positive electrode for hybrid electrochemical capacitors. The introduction of CDs is found to change the PBA morphologies by slowing down the precipitation rates. Besides, the electrolyte type also affects the performance of the composite electrode. Under the best conditions (moderate CDs content and hybrid electrolyte of 1.3 M Na2SO4 and 0.5 M KOH), the CDs/NiFe PBA NCs-NF electrode exhibits enhanced capacitance properties and stability compared to the counterpart electrodes. In addition, the assembled HEC with the CDs/NiFe PBA NCNF composite electrode presents wide potential window, remarkable energy density and power density, as well as good cycling stability, which can be attributed to the accelerated electrolyte transport and the improved electrolyte/electrode interaction caused by the 3D macroporous structure of NF and the unique electrochemical properties of CDs. The coupling of morphology tailoring with composition modulation in this work will bring a promising way to push the development of PBA-based materials in HEC field or even other related energy areas.

Zhirong Guo: Writing – original draft, Visualization. Rongbin Song: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Lin Zhang: Methodology, Conceptualization, Supervision. Zhaohui Li: Funding acquisition. Hongchang Yao: Formal analysis. Qingchao Liu: Resources, Software. Jianshe Wang: Validation. Zhongjun Li: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21974125).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.01.056.

ce:section.ce:para:
